region_of_origin: Italy (Calabria for nduja; Lazio for arrabbiata)
food_subgroup: Pasta dish with cured pork product

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for arrabbiata pasta with nduja. FOODON_00002473 (pasta food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers pasta dishes broadly; no specific arrabbiata-with-nduja term exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept maps specifically to arrabbiata pasta with pork nduja.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of spicy arrabbiata pasta with pork nduja is approximately 350 g, comprising a full pasta portion with sauce and nduja spread incorporated.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Pasta with tomato sauce and meat (SR Legacy / Foundation Foods composite); supplemented with USDA data for nduja-style cured pork sausage (Italian pork sausage, cooked)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact USDA or HPB entry exists for arrabbiata pasta with pork nduja. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for pasta with tomato-based meat sauce (FDC ID 172152 and related entries) with USDA data for Italian-style pork sausage (FDC ID 167904) to represent the nduja component. The dish is a spicy tomato-based pasta with a high-fat cured pork spread; macros reflect a weighted composite at approximately 60% pasta-with-tomato-sauce and 40% nduja-enriched sauce by caloric contribution. Sodium is elevated due to nduja's cured nature. Values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the combined dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — pasta (cooked, al dente) analogous estimate; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, et al.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for arrabbiata pasta with pork nduja. GI of 45 is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked pasta (spaghetti/penne, al dente, white wheat flour), which ranges GI 38–52 with a representative value of approximately 45. The tomato-based sauce and fat content from nduja are expected to lower the effective GI relative to plain pasta. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (45 × 20.5 g per 100g × 3.5 serving factor) / 100 = 45 × 71.75 / 100 ≈ 32.3; rounded to 40.8 using 350g serving: (45 × (20.5 × 3.5)) / 100 = (45 × 71.75) / 100 = 32.3 — corrected: GL = (45 × 71.75) / 100 = 32.3 g. Note: GL per serving = (glycemic_index × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (45 × 71.75) / 100 = 32.3. Value set to 32.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from cured pork nduja and seasoning
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its estimated sodium content of approximately 520 mg per 100 g (approximately 1,820 mg per standard 350 g serving), which represents a substantial portion of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from refined pasta carbohydrates
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of portion size, as the refined pasta base contributes a moderate glycemic load (estimated GL ~32 per serving); choosing al dente cooking and pairing with the fat and protein from nduja may attenuate the postprandial glucose response, but portion control remains essential.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat and sodium from pork nduja
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly, as pork nduja is a high-fat cured meat contributing approximately 3.1 g saturated fat per 100 g and elevated sodium (~520 mg/100 g), both of which are associated with increased cardiovascular risk when consumed in excess.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spicy Arrabbiata with Pork Nduja is an Italian pasta dish combining the classic Roman arrabbiata sauce — a fiery tomato-based sauce made with garlic, dried chilli peppers, and olive oil — with pork nduja, a spreadable, intensely spiced Calabrian cured pork sausage. The dish typically uses penne or spaghetti pasta cooked al dente, tossed in a sauce of cherry tomatoes, house-made chilli oil, dried chillies, and fresh basil, with nduja melted into the sauce to add richness, depth, and a pronounced spicy-savoury flavour. The nduja dissolves into the tomato base, releasing its fat and paprika-forward heat, creating a glossy, deeply flavoured sauce with a bold, smoky, and peppery character. Texturally, the dish offers firm pasta coated in a silky, slightly oily sauce. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~195 kcal/100 g) with meaningful protein (~8.5 g/100 g) from the pork nduja, moderate carbohydrates (~20.5 g/100 g) from the pasta, and elevated fat (~9.2 g/100 g) and sodium (~520 mg/100 g) due to the cured meat. Clinically, the high sodium and saturated fat content make it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease, while the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for those with Type 2 Diabetes. This dish is served at Mingle - Funan in Singapore and is priced at SGD 28.50.
common_user_queries: Is nduja pasta high in fat?

confidence_reason: No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or USDA FoodData Central for arrabbiata pasta with pork nduja as a combined dish. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy composite data for pasta with tomato-meat sauce and Italian pork sausage. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database pasta entries. Confidence is medium due to the estimation approach.
notes: Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA composite source data for this dish type. All other -1 fields are not applicable or not reported in the source database for this estimated composite. Nutrient values represent a plausible per-100g estimate for the combined dish based on USDA SR Legacy data; actual values may vary depending on pasta type, nduja quantity, and preparation method. Glycemic load per serving corrected to 32.3 based on calculation: (GI 45 × 71.75 g carbohydrate per 350g serving) / 100 = 32.3. The dish is not halal due to pork content. No Malay, Tamil, or Hindi local names are provided as this is an Italian dish served in Singapore and not a Singaporean dish; the multilingual naming rules for Singaporean dishes do not apply. Chinese transliterations are provided as courtesy names for Chinese-speaking diners in Singapore.
